meta.title: Localized field
meta.description: A localized field is a single component that enables users to input and manage content for multiple language versions of a piece of data within one unified form area.

guidelines.mdx:
## Guidelines

Localized field guidelines ensure the consistent design, accessibility, and
efficient management of content that requires simultaneous input and storage
across multiple defined language versions.

### Best practices

- **Prioritize default locale:** Always ensure the primary or default locale is
  easily accessible and the first field a user sees.
- **Use clear flags/labels:** The language switcher controls must clearly and
  concisely indicate the active locale to prevent accidental input into the
  wrong language version.
- **Maintain content consistency:** The underlying input component chosen for
  renderItem must remain the same for every single locale instance to ensure
  data integrity and predictable behavior.
- **Handle text expansion:** Design the surrounding layout and container with
  enough horizontal space to accommodate text expansion in wordier languages
  like German or French.
- **Provide contextual tools:** If the underlying input is complex (like a rich
  text editor), ensure localization features (like spellcheck based on the
  active locale) are supported.

### Usage

A localized field's usage is to efficiently capture and manage content that must
be translated and stored for multiple language versions within a single form.

> [!TIP]\
> When to use

- **Product descriptions/titles:** For any field that must be translated for
  customer-facing display (e.g., product titles, category names, SEO keywords).
- **Multiple language input:** When a user (typically a content manager or
  merchant) is responsible for entering content for all supported markets from
  one interface.
- **Structured metadata:** For input fields that hold localized metadata (e.g.,
  unique localized pricing tiers, currency symbols, SEO, or tax explanations).

> [!CAUTION]\
> When not to use

- **Non-translatable data:** When capturing internal IDs, SKUs, inventory
  counts, or purely numerical data that does not change by language. Use a
  standard Text Input or Number Input instead.
- **Single-language input:** If the application supports only one language, or
  if the user is only ever responsible for one market's content.
- **Large, multi-page content:** For entire pages or long-form documentation
  that would require a complex translation workflow; use a dedicated content
  management system instead.

### Do’s and don’ts for the localized field

> [!TIP]\
> **Do**
>
> - Ensure switching languages preserves the content of the input field
>   currently being edited, allowing easy comparison.
> - If needed, you can include a visual indicator with a country or language
>   code on the active input field for clarity.

> [!CAUTION]\
> **Don't**
>
> - Don't hide the language switcher interface completely unless an external
>   system (like a global header) explicitly manages it.
> - Don't allow the underlying input component to change across locales (e.g.,
>   using a text input for English and a text area for French).
> - Don't allow users to input data into a locale that is not defined in the
>   component's locales variable.

toc.value: Do’s and don’ts for the localized field
